sentences.text: Yes.
It was still in Turkey, when I was in good position, so I easily told the consul that I would wait.
Then, when the catastrophe came, I thought, I have a visa to America waiting for me.
I can go.
So I went to the consul.
That is where I had my first contact with the consul in Marseilles.
And then he heard the story about what happened, so he interviewed me.
I mentioned that already.
But he said, your visa is ready, but the affidavit from your aunt is no good anymore.
It's too old.
You have to write to her and tell her to renew it.
OK, I'll write to her.
And this wonderful aunt was afraid to be responsible for a whole family.
I was [INAUDIBLE].
She didn't answer.
So I wrote to my friend with whom I had trouble as a youngster.
We didn't talk for a year to each other because of his behavior.
It was a little chauvinistic behavior.
And I said, would you be so kind?
You live in Kansas City.
Kansas City is around the corner.
What did I care where New York and Kansas City were?
I thought it was around the corner.
He goes there and tells her, what is the matter with you?
Why don't you send the affidavit?
So he said, don't worry.
I'll send the affidavit.
He was a young-comer too.
I don't think that he was yet a citizen, but his brother was already a citizen, and his brother was in the fur business.
He was a friend of mine too, so he gave me the affidavit.
He says, forget the aunt, and I'll send you the affidavit.
So he saved, really, our life.
Here comes the affidavit, but in the meantime, I was in Marseilles, and the war started.
I have the paper.

Every citizen of the Reich had to appear, with food for one day, and clothes, to go in the prison camp, because being a citizen of the Reich, he had to be-- so I didn't feel like a citizen of the Reich because I am Austrian.
I came with an Austrian passport.
No, I came to Turkey with an Austrian passport, but when I wanted to go out of Turkey, Austria was already occupied.
So there was no Austria.
So I had to take a German passport, and I came with the German passport to Marseilles, which made me a citizen of the Reich.
But I ignored it.
I am not a German.
I don't have to listen to the orders that the citizens of the Reich had to do.
But the police somehow knew there were many Jewish refugees who are in the same condition, who are Austrian, but really they were not Austrian and they are not Germans.
So what they did to do to get them all we had the [NON-ENGLISH] HIAS took care of us, gave us a room to live, gave us food, and we had to go to the soup kitchen.
Every day, to the soup kitchen.
And one day when I went with my oldest son-- my wife that day stayed in the hotel with the other kid, and I went alone with him.
And there the police had arrested all the Jews in the soup kitchen who were with a German passport.
And I had the kid.
They arrested me with the kid, and nobody could leave the hall.
In the meantime, my wife heard about it.
They notified my wife to pick up the kid, but she couldn't enter the room.
My oldest son was with me, and the police came and took him from me.
And he clings to me and wrestles with the police.
Today he says that was when he started to hate police.
They broke his hand and took him off of me and took him out to the mother, so I was arrested and put in a big bus.
And they were all Jews, and there were many Jews, Hassidims, you know, with the payots and a long caftan.
And on the French camion, the French bus, there were two soldiers in front, and one with the French flag, and would whistle, and they are taking us through Marseilles.
The first German prisoners.
The Jews with the payots, the first German prisoners.
We couldn't explain, we are not Germans, we are Jews.
We are better enemies of Hitler then you have.
You are going to prison.
So I was two years in a real prison, and from there, they took me in the camp.
But before I talk about that, I would like to talk about the care HIAS took of us.
They paid for the hotel.
Still, as I said, I got some money from my friend, but it was not enough.
A certain amount from Switzerland from the mother of the architect, so that was how we survived with this little money.
Also, I could take a job from a businessman who had stores.
They wanted advertising on the big glass windows, so I made the lettering, whatever they wanted to say, and I cut it out and different colors that they wanted.
And at night, I went out and glued it on the windows.
So I made a little money.
Other jobs that were not enough.
Yeah.
As I said, the HIAS took care of us, and it was a very hard life.
What were things like in the prison?
It was easier in prison-- it was easier to take than in the hotel room where my wife with the-- for instance, we had no heat-- cold.
We had no way to make warm water.
The baby was still one year old.
We had a little burner with alcohol.
The alcohol doesn't even-- it took such a long time until a pot with water to make it warm, to wash the kid and to wash the diapers.
And then when she hung up the diapers in the evening, in the morning when she needed it, it was frozen.
It was frozen in the-- This woman, what she went through, and always smiling.
Always smiling and not complaining.
A heroine, really.
And it was that terrible.
It was no comparison to what her sisters went through in the German concentration camp, but what she went through, and always smiling.
Anyway, she was left alone with the two kids, and I went to prison, knowing that prisons-- prison-- I never had it-- we had it very good there.
What do you want, we had-- there was a concentration of German culture, German-Jewish culture.
There were actors.
There were men of law, men of religion-- rabbis.
There were-- we had lectures.
We had a university there.
Lectures.
We had theater, actors, you know.
It was unbelievable, the cultural life in the prison camp.
But there were also the German legionnaires, you know.
They were also, because France said, we hired you as soldiers, but you are still-- you are still Germans, so you are going to prison too.
So we had those anti-Semites, those legionnaires, but they separated us.
We were in an old, in a factory-- in a brick factory.
They were catacombs.
It was really called catacombs.
I have a picture of the catacombs there.
They gave us a place with hay where you slept.
In the German concentration camp, they had beds.
We slept on the ground on hay, but it was not so bad.
Yeah, and these legionnaires when came Jewish holidays, they made-- they arranged all with bricks.
They arranged.
Did they have a Torah?
I don't know where they had the Torah.
And it was a big factory with several things, and up there were the legionnaires.
The y lived up there, and during the service, they threw bricks down.
So one wasn't safe even there.
The food, it was better than in the soup kitchen of the [NON-ENGLISH] although [NON-ENGLISH] had healthy food.
It was only every day the same.
It was potatoes and eggs, and then the next day was eggs and potatoes.
It was always the same.
And soup, we had a big soup.

We had from the restaurant-- there was a time we went to the restaurant and ate there, but then the hall was moved, and we had to take the soup and the food-- we had to bring it from the restaurant to the hall.
And whoever was volunteering to carry the soup got the bones which were in the soup.
And the kids liked the bones.
That has nothing to do with the camp.
In the camp we had good food.
We had even wine.
What do you want?
A Frenchman cannot lived without wine.
My oldest boy went to a French school.
He got a little-- no, not he.
His mother didn't give him any.
But other kids came with a little bottle of wine-- children.
Five years-- How long how long were you in the prison?
In the prison?
I believe, I have to-- '39 till '40.
One year.
Now what happened in the prison?
My wife and the kids were in the hotel.
HIAS took care of them.
So I was in the prison.
And when I came to-- I was arrested.
I had nothing.
So the good wife-- and they took me to Les Milles, 30 kilometers away.
So she knew that I had nothing to wear, no coat, so she brought me my winter coat, and she brought me a blanket.
We had no blankets, only hay.
That was when she visited me the first time.
Then she came another time to visit with my oldest son.
So in the camp, as I said, it was a great time.
It really was a university.
I wouldn't like to have it missed.
First of all, the cooperation of the people, and the education that you got there, and all the lectures, theater, music.
You had everything what you-- cabaret. .
There is a book by Feuchtwanger.
He was a very famous German writer.
He wrote a book.
I was with him in the same camp.
Anyway.
Les Milles.
It was a famous camp.
And I have pictures in the catacombs and the-- yeah, so--
About the camp life, there were 3,000 people, and there were only toilet facilities.
There were toilet facilitites at the beginning, maybe there were 10 toilet facilities.
No way to wash yourself, if you wanted wash your-- I was a fanatic in washing myself.
I had to have my bath every day.
And there were three-- what do you call it?
Faucets.
Three faucets in different places in the whole camp, outside, freezing.
But I took my shower, believe it-- crazy-- I took my shower every morning with the ice-cold water, until the Red Cross came and said no sanitary facilities.
Then they had to build latrines.
They had to build a big boilers-- hot water.
We had hot and cold water.
Before, as I said, there were 10 toilets for the brick workers, you know?
It was a brick factory, but there were not 3,000 people.
And the people were sick.
They had all diarrhea.
It was terrible.
The seats were all dirty, so what did people do?
They couldn't sit down because it was full of dirt, so they stepped on and stayed on the-- on the board, you know?
To avoid that, the commander ordered that all the seats in this-- there should be barbed wire so people would not be able to sit there.
So what did we do?
We stepped on the top of the wire.
It was really terrible, and everybody was sick, with permanent diarrhea.
But that was really the bad thing in the camp.
Otherwise, it was a university and a cabaret.
And money-- I didn't have money, but whenever I wanted to have money, I could-- there were people there, rich people, they could get their money out from Germany somehow.
They had money, and they were interred up to 65 years.
All people under 65 had to go in the camp.
And they had money, but their socks got holes, so I mended the holes for the socks and got a little money.
And I had always-- yeah, OK.
How I got out.
Then I met an old friend.
Today he's a famous writer, Austrian writer.
He's still alive, and he still writes.
I met him there.
I met Feuchtwanger.
Famous people.
The Austrian writer-- what was his name?
Drach.
Dr.
Drach.
And I'm still in correspondence with him.
Then we are in camp, and the battles are going on.
And Hitler always more and more approaching to France and came to Petain.
The government of France was defeated.
But before the defeat, the government ordered that-- this was not the only prison camp where I was.
There were many others.
They ordered that all these prison camps shall be removed from south France to the Spanish border, because the government wanted to save the prisoners.
I don't know if it was for humanitarian reasons.
Maybe for a humanitarian reason, because they were mostly Jews.
They would go directly in the oven.
And also to have prisoners for exchange.
So all the camps are going through the French border.
But in the mean-- wait a minute.
In the meantime, before that, to relieve the pressure which existed in this camp.
There were too many people, so they took parts in another camp, another place, Lambesc, it was called, and I was lucky to be one of them to be transferred to Lambesc.
Oh, beautiful it was there.
We had beds.
We didn't sleep on the hay anymore, and it was very nice.
There I came in conflict with the camp regulations, because there were homosexuals, you know, German.

They were expelled from-- they couldn't-- whether they were Jews or not, they were persecuted, so we had not only Jews in the camp, but also Germans who were either socialists or homosexuals or people who just couldn't stand it, decent German people who couldn't stand it.
And you know that the HIAS, the Jewish HIAS took care of these people too?
They didn't ask their religion.
You are a refugee, you could prove you were a refugee, he was accepted by the HIAS.
